a: Human-AI hybridity is the condition in which cognitive tasks — memory, attention, reasoning, navigation — are distributed across a human brain and external AI systems. The extended mind thesis (Clark and Chalmers, 1998) established that cognition routinely extends beyond the skull into notebooks and tools. Smartphones and AI assistants are the current frontier: when you outsource a decision to an algorithm, that algorithm becomes part of your cognitive system, shaping what you notice and what you choose.

q: Are we already cyborgs because of AI?
a: In the functional sense, yes. A cyborg is not defined by hardware implanted in the body but by cognition that depends on external systems to operate normally. Most people today navigate, remember, and decide in ways that break down without their devices. That dependency is the merger. Which parts of your thinking are yours, and which are the algorithm's? That question is not rhetorical anymore.

q: What did philosophers Clark and Chalmers say about the extended mind?
a: In their 1998 paper 'The Extended Mind,' Andy Clark and David Chalmers argued that cognitive processes need not be confined to the brain and body. They used the example of Otto, an Alzheimer's patient whose notebook functions as his memory — the information is consulted and used in exactly the same way internal memory would be. Their claim: if a device plays the same functional role as a mental state would, it qualifies as part of the mind, regardless of where it physically sits.

q: What does Mindkind say about human-AI cognitive communities?
a: Mindkind argues that we are now forming cognitive communities that span humans and machines — what it calls the Mindkind. These communities are not yet equal: processing speed, memory capacity, and network access create new stratifications. The essay 'A Day in the Stratified Mindkind' depicts this as a fiction, but the underlying gradient is real: those with access to better AI augmentation compound advantage in ways that traditional wealth inequality did not.
